name: 2° AV Block Mobitz II
aliases: Second Degree AV Block Type II
identifyBy: Sudden dropped QRS, P wave not followed by QRS (no prior PR prolongation)
Constant PR interval in all conducted beats
Often occurs in a regular ratio: 2:1, 3:1 (every 2nd or 3rd P wave drops)
Wide QRS in conducted beats (infranodal block - bundle of His or bundle branch)
High risk of progression to complete heart block

measurements.rate: Ventricular rate lower than atrial rate (depends on ratio)
measurements.pWaves: Present, regular; not all followed by QRS
measurements.prInterval: Fixed and constant in all conducted beats

management: Do NOT give atropine as this may paradoxically worsen 2:1 or high-grade block
Continuous monitoring: risk of sudden progression to complete heart block
IV access, resuscitation equipment immediately available
pearls: Unlike Mobitz I, Mobitz II does NOT warn before dropping (sudden block)
Atropine can worsen Mobitz II by increasing atrial rate without improving AV conduction ratio
The block is below the AV node (infranodal) - atropine acts on the AV node only
High-grade Mobitz II (2:1, 3:1) may mimic sinus bradycardia so check for non-conducted P waves
Mobitz II virtually always requires permanent pacemaker and it does not resolve spontaneously
differentials: Mobitz I (Wenckebach): PR lengthens before drop; narrow QRS
Complete heart block: no P waves conduct at all
Blocked PAC: premature, different P-wave morphology
2:1 AV block: cannot distinguish Mobitz I from II in 2:1 ratio

qaNotes: Constant PR intervals in conducted beats with intermittent non-conducted P waves; often wide QRS.
